skill.name: terraform-aws-modules
skill.description: Terraform module creation for AWS — reusable modules, state management, and HCL best practices. Use when building or reviewing Terraform AWS infrastructure.
skill.summary: Expert guidance for building production-ready Terraform modules on AWS with state management and HCL best practices.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 25 patterns but all are false positives. External command references are Terraform CLI documentation examples (terraform fmt, validate, plan, force-unlock), not executable code. The CIDR block 10.0.0.0/16 is standard RFC1918 private networking for VPC configuration. This is documentation-only content with no executable code.

content.value_statement: Creating reusable Terraform infrastructure is complex and error-prone. This skill provides expert guidance on module design, state management, and production HCL patterns for AWS.
actual_capabilities: Design reusable Terraform module structures with variables, outputs, and main configurations
Configure S3 remote state backends with DynamoDB locking for team environments
Apply HCL best practices including for_each over count for stable resource identity
Implement consistent resource tagging using default_tags provider blocks
Review Terraform code for security and production readiness
Troubleshoot state lock issues and migration challenges
limitations: Only supports AWS provider infrastructure, not multi-cloud setups
Does not cover AWS CDK or CloudFormation alternatives
Requires existing Terraform and AWS knowledge for effective use
Does not execute Terraform commands, only provides guidance

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Building VPC Modules
use_cases.description: Create a reusable VPC module with proper variable definitions, outputs, and tagging strategy for consistent network deployments across environments.

use_cases.title: Platform Team Setting Up State Management
use_cases.description: Configure S3 backend with DynamoDB locking for production Terraform state, ensuring safe concurrent operations and state encryption.

use_cases.title: Developer Migrating from CloudFormation
use_cases.description: Translate existing CloudFormation templates to Terraform HCL with proper module structure and state management from the start.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Module Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Terraform module structure for an AWS S3 bucket with versioning enabled. Include variables.tf for bucket name and versioning flag, main.tf with the resource, outputs.tf for bucket ARN and name, and versions.tf pinning the AWS provider.

prompt_templates.title: Review Module for Best Practices
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Terraform module for best practices and security issues. Check for proper variable validation, sensitive output marking, provider version pinning, and consistent tagging strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Design Remote State Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a remote state backend configuration for a team with dev, staging, and production environments. Use S3 for storage with DynamoDB locking. Include separate state keys per environment and enable encryption.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate from Count to For_Each
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Terraform configuration to use for_each instead of count for managing multiple subnets. The current code uses count with list indexing which causes issues when removing middle elements. Preserve all existing attributes.

output_examples.input: Create a reusable EKS cluster module with proper variable definitions
output_examples.output: Provides a complete module structure with variables.tf defining cluster_name, node_count, and instance_type with validation rules, main.tf with aws_eks_cluster and aws_eks_node_group resources using for_each for node groups, outputs.tf exporting cluster endpoint and node group ARN, and versions.tf pinning aws provider to >= 4.0

output_examples.input: Configure state locking for production environment
output_examples.output: Returns S3 backend configuration with bucket terraform-state-prod, key env/prod/terraform.tfstate, region us-east-1, DynamoDB table tf-state-lock, encryption enabled, plus instructions for initial state import and team access policies

best_practices: Pin provider and module versions in versions.tf to prevent breaking changes during updates
Use for_each instead of count when resource identity must remain stable across applies
Store remote state in S3 with DynamoDB locking and enable server-side encryption
anti_patterns: Using count for resources where middle elements may be removed, causing unnecessary recreation
Committing .tfstate files to version control which exposes sensitive resource data
Referencing state from other configurations without using terraform_remote_state data source

faq.question: How do I handle state lock errors after a failed Terraform apply?
faq.answer: First verify no other operations are running. Then use terraform force-unlock with the lock ID from the error message. Always confirm the previous operation truly failed before forcing unlock.

faq.question: Should I use one state file or separate states per environment?
faq.answer: Use separate state files per environment (dev, staging, prod) with different keys in the same S3 bucket. This provides isolation while keeping state management simple and enables independent lifecycle per environment.

faq.question: How do I manage secrets in Terraform variables?
faq.answer: Mark sensitive variables with sensitive = true in variables.tf. Store actual values in AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store and reference them via data sources. Never commit variable values with secrets to version control.

faq.question: What is the difference between count and for_each in Terraform?
faq.answer: Count uses numeric indices which shift when elements are removed, causing resource recreation. For_each uses stable string or object keys, preserving resource identity when other elements change. Prefer for_each for most use cases.

faq.question: How do I import existing AWS resources into Terraform state?
faq.answer: Define the resource in your HCL first, then run terraform import with the resource address and AWS resource ID. For complex imports, consider using terraform import with -generate-config to auto-generate the configuration.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for Azure or GCP infrastructure?
faq.answer: This skill specializes in AWS provider patterns. While general Terraform concepts apply to Azure and GCP, provider-specific resources and best practices differ. Use provider-specific guidance for non-AWS infrastructure.
